comment_text.sn3.7:1.3: In the previous Sutta, Pasenadi lamented the corrupting influence of wealth. Here he talks of how he sees this corruption in his court. he speaks further of his frustrations at MN 98:13.1. | Read aḍḍa (variants attha, aṭṭa) which in Sanskrit has the senses “solve, infer, argue, discern”. The commentary interprets aḍḍakaraṇa as the “place of judgement” i.e. the court, which is clearly the case at DN 17:1.11.1.
comment_text.sn3.7:1.5: Bhadramukha is used here by by a king of his grown son. Elsewhere, by a family for a child (MN 40:6.2); by lay people addressing monks (MN 81:21.1); by a laywoman addressing a deity (AN 7.53:3.4);  (SN 3.7:1.5); and, with the addition of the familiar tāta, by a brahmin wife to her husband (MN 100:3.5). The term is of Sanskritic form and is found commonly in the Avadāna literature. Turning to later Sanskrit sources, Nāṭyaśāstra 19.12 says it should be used when addressing inferiors, while Sāhityadarpaṇa 6.154 says it is used when addressing a prince (see SN 3.7 above). The early Pali usage, however, suggests it was an affectionate and respectful term of somewhat elevated usage, but not restricted by status. Since bhadda by itself is a common form of address in the sense “my dear”, I think the suffix -mukha has its intensive sense here, “dearest”, rather than “dear face”.

root_text.sn3.7:1.2: Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho rājā pasenadi kosalo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn3.7:1.3: “idhāhaṁ, bhante, aḍḍakaraṇe nisinno passāmi khattiyamahāsālepi brāhmaṇamahāsālepi gahapatimahāsālepi aḍḍhe mahaddhane mahābhoge pahūtajātarūparajate pahūtavittūpakaraṇe pahūtadhanadhaññe kāmahetu kāmanidānaṁ kāmādhikaraṇaṁ sampajānamusā bhāsante.
root_text.sn3.7:1.5: ‘alaṁ dāni me aḍḍakaraṇena, bhadramukho dāni aḍḍakaraṇena paññāyissatī’”ti.
root_text.sn3.7:2.2: Yepi te, mahārāja, khattiyamahāsālā brāhmaṇamahāsālā gahapatimahāsālā aḍḍhā mahaddhanā mahābhogā pahūtajātarūparajatā pahūtavittūpakaraṇā pahūtadhanadhaññā kāmahetu kāmanidānaṁ kāmādhikaraṇaṁ sampajānamusā bhāsanti;
root_text.sn3.7:2.3: tesaṁ taṁ bhavissati dīgharattaṁ ahitāya dukkhāyā”ti.

translation_text.sn3.7:1.2: Seated to one side, King Pasenadi said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn3.7:1.3: “Sir, when I’m sitting in court I see well-to-do aristocrats, brahmins, and householders—rich, affluent, and wealthy, with lots of gold and silver, lots of property and assets, and lots of money and grain. But they tell deliberate lies for the sake of sensual pleasures.
translation_text.sn3.7:1.5: ‘Enough with passing judgment today. Now my dearest will be known by the judgments he makes.’”
translation_text.sn3.7:2.1: “That’s so true, great king! That’s so true!
translation_text.sn3.7:2.2: Those who are well-to-do aristocrats, brahmins, and householders tell deliberate lies for the sake of sensual pleasures.
translation_text.sn3.7:2.3: That is for their lasting harm and suffering.”
translation_text.sn3.7:2.4: That is what the Buddha said. …
translation_text.sn3.7:3.1: “Full of desire for possessions and pleasures,
translation_text.sn3.7:3.3: they don’t notice that they’ve gone too far,
translation_text.sn3.7:3.4: like fish entering a net set out.
translation_text.sn3.7:3.6: for the result will be bad for them.”

variant_text.sn3.7:1.3: aḍḍakaraṇe → atthakaraṇe (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed); aṭṭakaraṇe (pts2ed)
variant_text.sn3.7:2.1: “Evametaṁ, mahārāja, evametaṁ, mahārāja → etthantare pāṭho si, pts1ed-2 potthakesu
